title: About Titanic AutoML
tagline: How a 1912 disaster, AutoML model selection, and an imbalanced binary classification problem come together on pandyaHomeLab.

sections.title: The Problem
body: The Titanic dataset (891 passengers from the 1912 disaster) is the canonical introduction to **imbalanced binary classification**. Each row is one passenger with seven features — class, sex, age, siblings/spouses aboard, parents/children aboard, fare, port of embarkation — and the label is whether they survived.

About **38%** of passengers survived. That imbalance matters: a lazy model that predicts "died" for everyone scores 62% accuracy without learning anything. The whole game is choosing a metric that doesn't fall for that trick.

sections.title: Approach — Why AutoML?
body: **PyCaret** automates the model-selection step: train a panel of candidate algorithms, cross-validate each, rank by a chosen metric, then pick a winner. We compare seven classifiers — LR, Random Forest, XGBoost, Decision Tree, KNN, Naive Bayes, SVM — and let AUC pick the best.

bullets.label: Why AUC is the primary metric
bullets.text: On imbalanced data, accuracy lies. AUC measures whether the model can rank survivors *above* non-survivors, regardless of where you draw the survival threshold. It's threshold-independent.

bullets.label: Why limit to 7 algorithms
bullets.text: PyCaret's default trains 18+ classifiers. With `n_jobs=1` (required to avoid PyCaret's `ThreadLocalVariable` issues) that took >2 min — past Nginx's read timeout. Seven is the sweet spot.

bullets.label: Why finalize_model
bullets.text: After picking the winner from cross-validation, we retrain it on **all** the data — training plus validation. That's the model that ships.

bullets.label: Why we bypass predict_model
bullets.text: PyCaret's `predict_model()` looks up its experiment in thread-local storage. FastAPI's thread pool gives us a different thread per request, so we call the underlying sklearn Pipeline directly.

sections.title: Architecture
body: Same per-project 4-layer architecture (ADR-013) as the other ML demos — but with one twist: **db-logic persists the dataset to PostgreSQL** on first load. Subsequent container restarts read from the database, not from seaborn.

sections.title: Network & Deployment
body: Lives in the ML domain alongside iris-knn and housing-linear. Unlike them, this one has a real database dependency — the container talks to ml-postgres on the same network for persistent dataset storage and to ml-mlflow for experiment logging.

items.name: FastAPI 0.104
items.purpose: Async HTTP API framework — but `/predict` is sync (PyCaret threading)

items.name: Pydantic v2
items.purpose: Strict validation — `@model_validator` enforces the 1st-class fare floor business rule

items.name: scikit-learn
items.purpose: Underlying classifiers (LR, RF, DT, KNN, NB, SVM)

items.name: XGBoost + LightGBM
items.purpose: Gradient-boosted decision tree members of the comparison panel

items.name: PostgreSQL
items.purpose: Persistent storage for the cleaned Titanic dataset

items.name: MLflow 3.x
items.purpose: Manual run logging — params, metrics, tags (no auto-logging due to deepcopy issues)

items.name: Synology DSM
items.purpose: Host OS — Docker engine, persistent volumes

sections.title: Read the Code Walkthrough
body: The **ML Project Walkthrough** uses iris-knn as the worked example (~1,200 lines), but the pattern applies here too. The interesting differences in titanic-automl are the AutoML pattern (PyCaret), the PostgreSQL persistence twist, and several PyCaret-specific gotchas hard-won during Phase 1d.

bullets.label: Project anatomy
bullets.text: Same 4-layer structure as iris-knn — file tree and patterns are identical.

bullets.label: Request flow
bullets.text: A `/predict` request traced hop-by-hop. Note: route handlers are `def`, not `async def`.

bullets.label: Per-layer patterns
bullets.text: Same factory pattern, ABC contracts, lazy init — read once, applies everywhere.

bullets.label: PyCaret gotchas
bullets.text: `pycaret[full]` causes pip resolver hell · pyyaml 5.4.1 needs setuptools<65 · `log_experiment=False` to avoid deepcopy of ThreadLocalVariable · sync routes only.

bullets.label: MLflow integration
bullets.text: Manual logging this time — PyCaret's auto-logging trips on deepcopy of thread-local state.

links.label: Read the full walkthrough on GitHub

sections.title: Performance
body: Metrics come from PyCaret's 10-fold cross-validation across the seven candidate algorithms. The leaderboard is visible on the main page — what's below explains *why* AUC is the primary metric and what each one teaches.
**Key insight: a model predicting "died" for everyone scores 62% accuracy.** That's why we don't optimize for accuracy on imbalanced data.

metrics_detail.name: AUC
metrics_detail.what: Area Under the ROC Curve — the probability that a random survivor gets a higher predicted score than a random non-survivor.
metrics_detail.why_chosen: **The primary metric we optimized for.** AUC is *threshold-independent* — it measures the model's ability to rank, regardless of where you set the survival cutoff. Critical for imbalanced data: a model can have 70% accuracy by predicting the majority class while having no real ranking ability (AUC ≈ 0.5).
metrics_detail.watch_out: AUC = 0.5 means random guessing; below 0.5 means worse than random (and you can flip predictions to get above 0.5). AUC near 1.0 on training data is suspicious — likely overfitting.

metrics_detail.name: Accuracy
metrics_detail.why_chosen: Easy to communicate — but reported here as *context*, not as the optimization target. With ~62% mortality, predicting "all died" already scores 62%. Our model needs to beat that meaningfully to be worth anything.
metrics_detail.watch_out: **The classic trap on imbalanced data.** A 79% accuracy that comes from "always predict majority" is worthless. Always sanity-check accuracy against the majority-class baseline.

metrics_detail.name: F1
metrics_detail.what: Harmonic mean of precision and recall on the survivor class.
metrics_detail.why_chosen: Balances "did we falsely predict survival" (precision) against "did we miss real survivors" (recall). On imbalanced data, F1 is a much stronger signal than accuracy because it focuses on the minority class.
metrics_detail.watch_out: F1 weights precision and recall equally. If you cared more about one (e.g. search & rescue values recall over precision), use F-beta with β ≠ 1.

metrics_detail.name: Precision
metrics_detail.what: When the model predicts "survived", how often is it right?
metrics_detail.why_chosen: If a downstream action is taken on positive predictions (e.g. notifying families of survivors), precision tells you the false-positive risk. Higher = fewer false survivor predictions.
metrics_detail.watch_out: You can trivially maximize precision by only predicting "survived" for the very most confident cases — but recall plummets. Always paired with recall.

metrics_detail.name: Recall
metrics_detail.what: Of the actual survivors, what fraction did the model find?
metrics_detail.why_chosen: If the cost of *missing* a real survivor is high (the search-and-rescue analogy), recall dominates the metric choice. ~71% means we're catching most real survivors.
metrics_detail.watch_out: You can trivially maximize recall by predicting "survived" for everyone — but precision drops to 38% (the base rate). Always paired with precision.
